question_text: Your practice management team has reviewed the clinic-wide cancer screening rates and found that the frequency of cervical cancer screening appears to have fallen behind other cancer screenings. You are asked to lead a quality improvement project to increase rates of cervical cancer screening for appropriate patients. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate first step?
explanation: Quality improvement is a key area for clinical practice, but initiating these projects can be challenging. In order to plan appropriate interventions, it must first be determined what barriers are responsible for hindering performance in a certain area. If evaluation shows that there is a systemic issue in capturing data, rather than a clinician issue, no other interventions will be beneficial. The initial step should be delineating any current issues prior to implementing a change.
resource_link: Stone EG, Morton SC, Hulscher ME, et al. Interventions that increase use of adult immunization and cancer screening services: a meta-analysis. Ann Intern Med. 2002;136(9):641-651.
options: Establishing a system for reviewing charts and contacting patients who are overdue for cervical cancer screening
Evaluating barriers to cervical cancer screening and reporting
Placing posters with information about cervical cancer screening inside the clinic
Sending generalized reminders to patients who may qualify for cervical cancer screening
Sending generalized reminders to all clinicians regarding cervical cancer screening guidelines

question_text: You are seeing a 32-year-old male in the office. His most recent hemoglobin A1c was 6.1%, indicating prediabetes. Which one of the following is most likely to prevent the development of diabetes?
explanation: Intensive lifestyle and behavior modification programs, such as the National Diabetes Prevention Program, are low-cost and can reduce the development of diabetes, with one U.S. study demonstrating a 27% reduction over 15 years. These programs help patients set weight loss goals >7% of their baseline, typically achieved through a caloric reduction of 500-1000 kcal per day, with activity goals >150 minutes per week, including up to 75 minutes per week of strength training.
resource_link: American Diabetes Association Professional Practice Committee. 3. Prevention or delay of diabetes and associated comorbidities: standards of care in diabetes-2025. Diabetes Care. 2025;48(Supplement_1):S50-S58.
options: Intensive lifestyle and behavior change program
Reduction in daily caloric intake by 250 kcal per day
Strength training for 90 minutes per week

question_text: An otherwise healthy 47-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with pain and swelling of his right foot after sustaining an injury during a game of ultimate frisbee. He has significant pain and swelling of his right foot but is weight-bearing. His right lateral foot shows significant bruising and swelling. Sensation and circulation are intact. An x-ray reveals a 5th metatarsal shaft fracture. Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation: This patient's imaging reveals a fifth metatarsal shaft fracture, which should be managed with a boot or hard-soled shoe for 3-6 weeks with weight-bearing. Foot fractures are typically caused by an axial or twisting force on the foot or a crush injury. A short leg cast is not appropriate because it is used to treat proximal fifth metatarsal and tarsal bone fractures.
resource_link: Silver S, Williams E, Plunkett ML. Common foot fractures. Am Fam Physician. 2024;109(2):119-129.
options: Weight-bearing with a walking boot for 3-6 weeks
Non-weight-bearing with a walking boot for 3-6 weeks
Non-weight-bearing with a short leg cast for 6 weeks

question_text: A 78-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of cough and fever and a 2-hour history of confusion. Her vital signs include a temperature of 38.8 C (101.8 F) and a blood pressure of 72/44 mm Hg with a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 53 mm Hg. A chest x-ray shows left lower lobe pneumonia. She is given 2 L of lactated Ringer solution and started on broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis secondary to pneumonia. Her blood pressure 3 hours later is 82/46 mm Hg with a MAP of 58 mm Hg. Which one of the following is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?
explanation: This patient is in septic shock secondary to pneumonia. Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines recommend early goal-directed therapy (EGDT) consisting of initial fluid resuscitation with 30 mL/kg of crystalloid solution within the first 3 hours and appropriate antibiotics within 1 hour. The guidelines also recommend initiating vasopressor therapy with norepinephrine as the first-line agent if the mean arterial pressure remains <65 mm Hg after fluid resuscitation.
resource_link: Evans L, Rhodes A, Alhazzani W, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: international guidelines for management of sepsis and septic shock 2021. Critical Care Medicine. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143.

question_text: You are called to the emergency department (ED) to admit a 48-year-old female with a past medical history of hyperthyroidism who presented with agitation, palpitations, and diarrhea. The ED team diagnosed thyroid storm secondary to the abrupt discontinuation of the patient's methimazole 2 weeks ago due to a lapse in insurance. On examination, she is tachycardic and hypertensive but can sustain a conversation without dyspnea. Laboratory studies are significant for elevated free T3 and T4 levels and an undetectable TSH level. Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial step in management?
explanation: Thyroid storm is a medical emergency that must be treated promptly. The initial treatment for thyroid storm is propylthiouracil (PTU) or methimazole. Glucocorticoids (hydrocortisone and dexamethasone, not prednisone) can also be used to block the conversion of T4 to T3. While iodine is used to block the synthesis of new thyroid hormone, it should be started at least 1 hour after the initiation of PTU.
resource_link: Ross DS, Burch HB, Cooper DS, et al. 2016 American Thyroid Association guidelines for diagnosis and management of hyperthyroidism and other causes of thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid. 2016;26(10):1343-1421.
